title: Cleaning and storing a bee smoker
subtitle: The end-of-season jobs that decide whether the smoker lights first time next spring.
excerpt: A smoker put away dirty is a smoker that fails on the first hive of the year. The chimney sticks, the bellows perish, and the fuel chamber holds last season's tar. A walk through the end-of-season strip-down, the cool-soak that takes the creosote off, and the dry indoor store that keeps the leather supple.
sourceNotes: National Bee Unit Beekeeper's Toolkit, end-of-season equipment care. BBKA Bee Craft archive, smoker maintenance notes. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive. Pearson's storage notes still hold.

glossaryTerms.definition: A canister with a bellows that burns smouldering fuel (hessian, wood chips, pine needles) to calm bees during a hive inspection.
The dark tarry residue that builds up inside a smoker chimney and around the lid hinge as wood and hessian smoulder. Sticks the lid shut and clogs the air path if left.
The squeeze-pump on the side of the smoker that pushes air through the burning fuel. A leather panel between two wooden boards; the leather perishes if stored damp.
A sticky resin bees collect from tree buds. Ends up on every hive tool, every smoker hook, and every glove cuff by the end of an inspection.

recipeTools.notes: For the test relight at the end.
For scraping out the chamber and the chimney.

content.text: The smoker has done its work for the season. The last inspection is in, the fondant is on, the mouse guard is fitted. The smoker goes in the shed and stays there until spring. What it looks like in March depends entirely on what you did with it in October.

A smoker put away with last season's fuel in the chamber and tar on the chimney is the most common reason a spring inspection goes wrong. The lid sticks, the bellows give a wet squeak, the first puff is grey ash rather than cool blue smoke. Half an hour of end-of-season care saves an hour of swearing on the first warm day.
When to do this

After the last hive visit of the year. In southern England that is usually mid to late October, after the autumn syrup feed and the mouse guard go on. Pick a dry day. The bellows leather needs to come into a warm dry room afterwards, not back into a damp shed.
What you need
The smoker, with whatever fuel is still in it.
A bucket of cool water and a stiff bristle brush.
A hive tool to scrape out the chamber and the chimney.
Old newspaper to lay the parts on.
A small tin of beeswax or saddle soap for the bellows leather.
Cool the smoker first
A working
smoker

is hot enough through the chimney wall to scorch a wooden bench. Stand it on a slab or a paving stone for twenty minutes after the last inspection and let it die back. The fuel inside will smoulder on for another half-hour. Once the chimney is cool enough to grip in a bare hand at the base, the strip-down can start.
Empty the chamber
Tip the cold ash and any unburnt fuel into the compost or onto a fire-safe spot in the garden. Reach in with the long end of a
propolis

-coated hive tool and scrape the inner wall of the chamber. A season's worth of tar comes off in thin black flakes. The grate at the bottom of the chamber lifts out on most modern smokers; tap it clean against a hard edge and brush off the underside.
Clear the chimney
Open the lid and look down the chimney. The bore narrows toward the top and the
creosote

builds up there first. A stiff bottle brush run up and down the chimney clears most of it. For stubborn deposits, set the chimney part down in a bucket of cool water with a squeeze of washing-up liquid and leave it overnight. The next morning the brush takes the rest off. Rinse with clean water and stand it upside down to drain.

attrs.title: Why cool water, not boiling
attrs.body: Stainless steel and copper sheet both survive a cool soak fine. Boiling water sets some tar deposits harder rather than softening them, and the sudden heat shock through a thin gauge can warp the chimney seam. A long cool soak beats a hot dunk.

content.text: The bellows
The
bellows

is the part that perishes first. The leather panel between the two boards dries out in a hot summer shed and cracks at the fold lines; left damp it goes mouldy along the edge stitching. Both kill the airflow. Wipe the leather down with a barely damp cloth to lift any soot, let it dry indoors for an hour, then work a thin film of beeswax or saddle soap into the surface with the pad of a thumb. The leather should darken slightly and feel supple under finger pressure. Do this on every face, including where the leather folds against the boards.
Reassemble and test-light

Once the chimney is dry, drop the grate back into the chamber and refit the lid. Squeeze the bellows a few times to check air passes freely through the air port at the back. There should be no scraping noise from the lid hinge; if there is, a wipe of beeswax on the pivot quietens it. A short test-light with a small wad of
smoker
fuel proves the airflow is clear. Let the test fuel burn out fully before storing.
Where to store it

A dry shelf indoors. The bellows leather wants to overwinter in a stable temperature and low humidity, not in an unheated outhouse where condensation runs down the walls. A kitchen cupboard, a porch shelf, or a workshop with a low background heat all work. Stand the smoker upright with the lid open a few millimetres so the chamber dries through. A loose cotton cover keeps spider webs out.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: End-of-season smoker problems
attrs.intro: The most common faults the strip-down surfaces, and what to do about each.

items.cause: Creosote welded the hinge during the last cool-down.
items.fix: Drop the whole top section into a bucket of cool soapy water for an hour. The lid lifts off once the deposit softens.

items.cause: Leather has dried out and the wood boards are pressing the fold against itself.
items.fix: Work beeswax into the leather, open and close the bellows slowly twenty times. If the leather has cracked through, the bellows panel is a replacement part; most apiary suppliers sell it.

items.symptom: Air port at the back is plugged.
items.cause: Ash worked back into the port during the last few inspections.
items.fix: Push a stiff wire through the port from the inside out. Check the bellows board for the same blockage on the air-intake side.

items.symptom: Chimney bore narrowed at the top.
items.cause: Heavy creosote build-up across the season.
items.fix: Overnight cool soak followed by a bottle brush. If a stubborn ring of tar remains, a short wood-screw chucked into a cordless drill takes it down to bare metal.

items.symptom: Faint mouldy smell from the bellows.
items.fix: Wipe the leather with a clean cloth dipped in a 1:10 white-vinegar-and-water mix, dry indoors, then re-wax. Store somewhere warmer and drier this time.

content.text: Notes in the apiary log

One line is enough. The date of the strip-down, the parts that needed replacing, and any kit reorders for spring. Bellows leather, a spare grate, and a tin of saddle soap are the three items most often noted. By March the log line reminds you that the smoker is ready and where it is.
